chapters.label: Full lesson narration
chapters.script:
A shopkeeper carries a credit card balance of forty thousand rupees. She pays twelve thousand, spends eight thousand again, and incurs finance charges. The account did not simply amortise downward like a vehicle loan. It breathed: payment reduced the balance, new purchases rebuilt it, and charges created another stream.

Credit-card securitisation finances revolving receivables. During a revolving period, principal collections can purchase new eligible receivables rather than immediately paying down all investor principal. The collateral population changes as customers spend, pay, default, and generate fees.

That replenishment creates the central bargain. Investors receive exposure to a managed pool rather than only the accounts present on closing day. Eligibility criteria, seller obligations, concentration limits, performance tests, and early-amortisation triggers must restrain how the pool changes.

Separate the cash into principal collections and finance-charge collections. Principal generally comes from repayment of purchases or cash advances. Finance charges can include interest and specified fees. The waterfall may use those streams differently, so combining them into one revenue number can misstate both yield and repayment capacity.

The payment rate measures how much cardholders pay relative to receivables over a period. A high rate returns cash quickly and can support liquidity. A falling rate leaves balances outstanding longer and may signal borrower stress before charge-offs fully appear.

Portfolio yield measures finance charges and relevant fees generated by the receivables under the transaction definition. Yield supplies excess spread after funding costs, servicing, and other expenses. Promotional rates, fee waivers, regulation, customer mix, and competitive pricing can reduce it.

Charge-offs recognise balances considered uncollectable under the applicable policy. They reduce asset value and consume excess spread or enhancement. Charge-off timing matters because delayed recognition can make current performance look stronger while losses accumulate underneath.

Dilution arises when receivables are reduced for reasons other than credit loss, such as refunds, disputed transactions, fraud adjustments, or billing corrections. The customer may be willing and able to pay, yet the amount legally owed shrinks. Seller support or dilution reserves may address that risk under the documents.

Suppose the shopkeeper's card balance is current, but a fraudulent purchase is reversed. The receivable disappears without a borrower default. Now suppose she loses income and stops paying. The balance remains valid but uncollectable. A credible analysis keeps dilution and credit loss separate.

New receivables can improve or weaken the pool. If the card issuer changes underwriting, markets to riskier customers, raises limits aggressively, or changes product terms, future collateral may behave differently from the original accounts. Static historical averages cannot govern a pool whose contents are being replenished.

Seller concentration is unavoidable because one issuer usually generates and services the receivables. The transaction depends on its systems, account management, marketing, fraud controls, dispute handling, and ability to keep supplying eligible balances. Thousands of cardholders create obligor granularity while one seller remains a common operational dependency.

The revolving period continues only while stated conditions hold. Principal collections are recycled into new receivables, preserving the collateral balance and leaving notes outstanding. When the period ends normally, principal begins amortising the securities according to the documents.

Early amortisation ends that recycling before the planned date. A trigger may respond to falling yield, rising charge-offs, weak payment rates, seller failure, insufficient receivables, or another defined event. Principal collections then redirect toward investors instead of financing new card spending.

The trigger protects investors by shortening exposure, but it can create pressure on the issuer. Funding that supported a revolving receivables book begins to run off precisely when performance or market confidence has weakened. Protection for one side changes liquidity for the other.

Excess spread is the difference between portfolio income and specified costs and losses. It can absorb deterioration before funded enhancement is hit. Because it is generated over time, a sudden loss spike or sharp yield decline can overwhelm it before future spread arrives.

Reserve accounts, subordination, overcollateralisation, and seller interests can provide additional protection. Their value must be measured under the same stress that hurts the card pool. If charge-offs rise while payment rates fall and yield compresses, several protections can weaken together.

The United States asset-backed securities framework has extensive relevance to revolving receivables disclosure. It helps establish the categories an investor should examine: account characteristics, pool performance, servicing, seller activity, and transaction triggers. A framework cannot supply the current trust data for a specific card program.

Useful reporting follows receivables, cardholder payments, purchases, finance charges, fees, delinquencies, charge-offs, recoveries, dilution, yield, payment rate, seller additions, and trigger headroom. Definitions must remain consistent across periods or apparent trends may reflect reporting changes rather than customers.

Imagine payment rates fall from twenty percent to twelve percent while receivable balances remain stable because customers keep spending. Cash arrives more slowly, balances persist longer, and household leverage may be rising. If charge-offs have not yet moved, the payment-rate change can still be an early warning.

Now transfer the model to a digital wallet offering revolving merchant credit. Merchants repay a percentage of daily sales and can redraw within limits. The technology feels new, but replenishment, payment rate, yield, dilution, seller dependence, and early amortisation remain the controlling mechanics.

A revolving pool is not a sealed box of loans. It is a living inventory of payment rights. Credit-card securitisation works only when the rules governing what enters, what leaves, and when recycling must stop are as carefully understood as the balances already inside.
